title: Monotone Expanders -- Constructions and Applications
speaker: Zeev Dvir
affiliation: Princeton University; Member, School of Mathematics
abstract: A Monotone Expander is an expander graph which can be decomposed into a union of a constant number of monotone matchings, under some fixed ordering of the vertices. A matching is monotone if every two edges (u,v) and (u',v') in it satisfy u < u' --> v < v'. It is not clear a priori if monotone expanders exist or not. This is partially due to the fact that the natural application of the probabilistic method does not work in this special case.

proof_stages.label: Monotone-expander problem and probabilistic obstruction
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines ordered bipartite d-monotone graphs and vertex expansion for families, poses the problem of minimizing d, and contrasts these graphs with ordinary random expanders by observing that naive random monotone matchings do not expand.

proof_stages.label: Logarithmic-degree shift/Cayley construction
proof_stages.summary: Cyclic shifts are used as basic monotone matchings, and an expanding generating set for the abelian group Z/nZ gives an explicit monotone expander of degree O(log n), or polylogarithmic degree in strongly explicit form.

proof_stages.label: Replacement-product degree reduction
proof_stages.summary: The replacement product lowers the degree by replacing each vertex of a large monotone expander with a smaller one, preserves expansion and monotonicity under the lexicographic ordering, and can be iterated to reach iterated-logarithmic degree.

proof_stages.label: Constant-degree existence via Bourgain's continuous spectral-gap construction
proof_stages.summary: Bourgain's construction supplies finitely many smooth increasing self-maps of the unit interval that expand measurable sets, obtained from near-identity elements of an SL2(R) action with spectral gap, and then discretizes them to finite monotone expanders.

proof_stages.label: Application to dimension expanders
proof_stages.summary: After defining dimension expanders, each monotone map is converted into a 0-1 linear map copying coordinates along edges, and the pivot positions of vectors in a subspace expand exactly as in the monotone graph, forcing dimension growth over every field.

proof_stages.label: Application to d-page and d-pushdown expanders
proof_stages.summary: The talk reviews book/page and pushdown graphs and their separator motivation, then shows that a d-monotone bipartite expander becomes a d-pushdown expander by placing the two ordered sides consecutively so each monotone matching becomes nested on a page.

proof_stages.label: Open directions, limitations, and consequences
proof_stages.summary: The discussion asks for simpler graph-only or random constructions, compares other structured maps that may fail to expand, and raises questions about optimal expansion quality, spectral gaps, further applications, and implications for lower-bound approaches.

examples.name: Uniform random monotone matchings
examples.note: The speaker contrasts ordinary random expanders with a uniformly random union of monotone matchings, where the first half of the ordered vertices maps essentially to the first half, illustrating why naive random sampling fails.
examples.engagement_reason: He checks the uniform random monotone matching model against expansion, noting the first half maps only to roughly the first half up to about sqrt(n).

examples.name: Ordinary expander construction by product plus exhaustive search
examples.note: The talk recalls the standard shortcut of applying replacement products until only a tiny constant-degree expander is needed and then finding it by exhaustive search, illustrating why a simple nonconstructive existence proof would be useful in the monotone setting.
examples.engagement_reason: He runs through the ordinary expander construction using replacement products and a final exhaustive search, including how degree is reduced and why expansion is preserved.

passes.reason: The product-plus-exhaustive-search method is a standard positive construction of ordinary expanders, used as an analogy rather than a refutation.

examples.name: Random linear maps over finite fields
examples.note: A small number of random linear maps over a finite field is cited as likely giving dimension expansion by a union bound over subspaces, making the dimension-expander definition concrete as a pseudorandom linear-algebraic object.
examples.engagement_reason: He sketches the probabilistic verification that a few random linear maps over a finite field form dimension expanders by bounding failure for a fixed subspace and counting subspaces.
